skill.name: ai-agent-development
skill.description: Guided workflow for designing, implementing, orchestrating, and evaluating AI agents and multi-agent systems.
skill.summary: Plan and build AI agent systems with phased guidance for architecture, tools, memory, orchestration, and evaluation.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged Markdown backticks as Ruby shell execution and ordinary workflow words as weak cryptography indicators. Manual review found no executable scripts, shell commands, network access, credential handling, prompt injection, or data exfiltration behavior in SKILL.md.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Markdown Backticks Misread as Shell Execution
low_findings.description: The flagged locations are Markdown inline code spans, skill names, or fenced copy-paste prompt examples. They do not invoke Ruby, a shell, or any command interpreter, and no user-controlled input is executed.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Very high confidence because every cited location is Markdown documentation or a plain text prompt. No shell syntax, Ruby code, command runner, or executable file is present.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Weak Cryptography Substring Matches
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography alerts correspond to ordinary prose such as description, design, and related workflow terms. No cryptographic API, hashing function, cipher selection, or security-sensitive implementation appears in the file.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: High confidence because the reviewed lines contain workflow headings, checklist items, and skill references only. There is no evidence of MD5, SHA-1, DES, RC4, or other weak algorithm usage.

content.value_statement: AI agent projects often fail because architecture, tools, memory, and evaluation are planned separately. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code a phased workflow for designing and validating agent systems.
actual_capabilities: Breaks agent projects into design, implementation, orchestration, tool, memory, and evaluation phases.
References related skills for architecture, CrewAI, LangGraph, tools, memory, and evaluation work.
Provides copy-paste prompts for each workflow phase.
Lists quality gates for agent logic, tools, memory, orchestration, and evaluation.
Outlines a simple agent flow from user input through planning, tools, memory, and response.
limitations: Does not include executable scripts or framework-specific source code.
Depends on referenced skills that may not be installed in every environment.
Provides workflow guidance, not production security guarantees.
Does not configure cloud services, vector stores, or deployment infrastructure.

use_cases.title: Prototype an autonomous agent
use_cases.description: Use the phased checklist to define the agent purpose, capabilities, tools, memory, and evaluation criteria before implementation.

use_cases.title: Plan a multi-agent system
use_cases.description: Map roles, communication, orchestration, and delegation before choosing CrewAI, LangGraph, or another framework.

use_cases.title: Add agent quality gates
use_cases.description: Use the workflow to review memory, tool integration, orchestration, and evaluation coverage across an agent project.

prompt_templates.title: Define an agent concept
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the AI agent development workflow to help me define an autonomous agent for this goal: [goal]. Include purpose, capabilities, tools, memory needs, and success metrics.

prompt_templates.title: Choose an implementation path
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the workflow to compare single-agent and multi-agent implementation paths for [project]. Recommend a framework approach and explain the tradeoffs.

prompt_templates.title: Design orchestration and memory
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the workflow to design orchestration, state handling, memory, and tool integration for an agent system that must [requirements].

prompt_templates.title: Review an agent architecture
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the workflow as a review checklist for this agent architecture: [architecture]. Identify missing quality gates, weak evaluation coverage, and risky assumptions.

output_examples.input: I need a customer support agent that can answer policy questions and escalate difficult cases.
output: A phased plan covering agent purpose, required tools, conversation memory, escalation rules, and evaluation criteria.
A recommendation to start with a single agent before adding role-based coordination.
A quality gate list for policy accuracy, escalation reliability, and memory behavior.

output_examples.input: We want several agents to research, draft, and review technical documents.
output: A role map for research, drafting, review, and coordination agents.
An orchestration plan that separates task delegation, state tracking, and human review.
Evaluation checks for source quality, consistency, and final approval readiness.

output_examples.input: Review our agent plan before we build it.
output: A gap analysis across architecture, tools, memory, orchestration, and evaluation.
A prioritized list of design risks and missing quality gates.
Recommended next prompts for deeper architecture and evaluation work.

best_practices: Define agent goals, allowed actions, and success metrics before selecting a framework.
Test tool use, memory retrieval, and orchestration paths with realistic scenarios.
Add human review for high-impact actions, ambiguous results, and policy-sensitive decisions.
anti_patterns: Starting with a multi-agent design before one agent has a clear responsibility.
Adding tools without defining inputs, outputs, permissions, and failure handling.
Treating a demo conversation as proof that memory, orchestration, and evaluation are reliable.

faq.question: Does this skill generate production-ready agent code?
faq.answer: No. It provides workflow guidance, prompts, and review checkpoints. Implementation still depends on the chosen framework and project constraints.

faq.question: Which agent frameworks does it mention?
faq.answer: It references CrewAI for multi-agent systems and LangGraph for stateful workflow orchestration.

faq.question: Can I use it with Claude, Codex, and Claude Code?
faq.answer: Yes. The workflow is written as tool-agnostic guidance with prompts that work across those assistants.

faq.question: Does it include security controls for agent tools?
faq.answer: It prompts users to design tool interfaces and error handling, but it does not provide a complete security framework.

faq.question: What should I prepare before using it?
faq.answer: Prepare the agent goal, expected users, allowed tools, data sources, memory requirements, and evaluation criteria.

faq.question: Is this useful for existing agent projects?
faq.answer: Yes. Use it as a checklist to find gaps in architecture, orchestration, memory, tool integration, and evaluation.
